allergens: gluten (rice flour may be processed in shared facilities)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried turnip cake is approximately 100 g, equivalent to one to two pan-fried slices as served at a hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Turnip K8 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Turnip K8 (cr_id G00010401). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat and sugar values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for radish cake (USDA SR Legacy #11429 and composite dish analogues) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean fried turnip cake. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the closest analogous food: steamed/fried rice-flour-based dim sum products with high vegetable content, referenced against University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice flour-based savoury cakes (GI range 48–56). The dominant ingredient is turnip/jicama, which has a very low GI (~17 per University of Sydney GI Database). The rice flour binder raises the composite GI to an estimated 52. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (52 × 3.39) / 100 = 1.76, rounded to 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (18 mg/100 g) with potassium (82 mg/100 g) and calcium (114 mg/100 g) supporting vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Fried turnip cake is suitable for hypertensive patients in its plain form due to very low sodium content; however, patients should avoid versions served with high-sodium sauces or seasonings, and should confirm no added salt in preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.39 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~52), and dietary fibre (2.4 g/100 g) providing glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried turnip cake has a very low carbohydrate load and low estimated GI, making it a suitable low-glycaemic snack for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should avoid sweet dipping sauces or sugar syrup accompaniments that would significantly increase glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.19 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat (~0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and low energy density (21 kcal/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried turnip cake is a low-fat, low-calorie, cholesterol-free food that is appropriate for cardiovascular disease patients when prepared with minimal oil; patients should request pan-fried rather than deep-fried preparation to minimise added fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vegetable base rich in calcium (114 mg/100 g) and estimated vitamin C (~15 mg/100 g), with dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turnip cake is a low-calorie, anti-inflammatory food suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; calcium and vitamin C content support bone and immune health, particularly relevant for patients on corticosteroid therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turnip Cake (Fried), known in Singaporean Chinese as 萝卜糕 (Luo Bo Gao) or Chai Tow Kway, is a traditional savoury dim sum and hawker dish made primarily from shredded turnip or daikon radish bound with rice flour and steamed into a firm cake, then sliced and pan-fried until a golden, crispy exterior forms while the interior remains soft and slightly chewy. The dish is a staple of Singaporean hawker culture and is commonly served as a breakfast, snack, or teatime item. Nutritionally, it is exceptionally low in calories (21 kcal/100 g), very low in carbohydrates (3.39 g/100 g), and contains negligible fat (0.19 g/100 g), making it one of the most diet-friendly savoury snacks in the Singaporean food landscape. Dietary fibre at 2.4 g/100 g and a low estimated glycaemic index (~52) make it suitable for glycaemic management. Sodium is very low at 18 mg/100 g in its plain form. Calcium content is notably high at 114 mg/100 g, and iron at 1.63 mg/100 g. The dish is suitable for patients managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed in its plain, minimally seasoned form. Clinically, it functions as a low-GI, low-sodium, low-fat snack with meaningful fibre and mineral content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chai tow kway?
Is luo bo gao good for diabetics?
Is fried radish cake low GI?
Can hypertension patients eat turnip cake?
What is the carbohydrate content of turnip cake?
What is chai tow kway made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Turnip K8 (cr_id G00010401) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, sugar, and vitamin C are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for radish cake (USDA SR Legacy #11429 and composite dish analogues) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this item. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, documented transparently.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.03 g), monounsaturated fat (0.04 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09 g), and sugar (1.5 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for daikon radish (USDA #11429) and rice flour composite dish analogues, as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this item. 2. Vitamin C (15 mg/100 g) is an estimate based on known vitamin C content of raw daikon radish per USDA SR Legacy; actual value in the fried preparation may be lower due to heat degradation. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific fried turnip cake preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the base formulation. 5. The food_group has been corrected from 'Desserts' (input) to 'Snacks' as turnip cake is a savoury dish. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean fried turnip cake was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no trans fats expected in a traditionally pan-fried vegetable-rice flour preparation.
